questions.question:
The search engine's business model ____ around the fulcrum of trust.

A. revolves  B. plays  C. sinks  D. bursts
questions.to_find: The most appropriate word to complete the sentence about a business model centred on trust

understand.plain: The sentence uses 'fulcrum of trust' as a metaphor — a fulcrum is a pivot point. Something that rotates around a pivot 'revolves' around it. The blank requires a verb that collocates naturally with 'around the fulcrum'. 'Revolves around' is the standard English idiom for something that centres on or is driven by a particular idea.

keywords.explain: To orbit or centre on something; used idiomatically to mean 'is fundamentally based on'
keywords.example: Her whole argument revolves around the idea of fairness.

keywords.explain: The pivot point of a lever; metaphorically, the central or most important element
keywords.example: Trust is the fulcrum of any lasting relationship.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed for this vocabulary sentence-completion question.

given.aim: Select the verb that best fills the blank in the sentence about a business model and trust

terms.meaning: The plan by which a company creates and captures value
terms.example: A subscription business model revolves around recurring payments.
terms.connects: Subject of the sentence needing an appropriate verb

terms.meaning: Pivot or central support; here used metaphorically as 'core element'
terms.example: Customer satisfaction is the fulcrum of service quality.
terms.connects: The prepositional object that pairs with 'revolves around'

terms.meaning: Words that naturally go together in standard usage
terms.example: 'Make a decision' is a natural collocation; 'do a decision' is not.
terms.connects: Guides the choice: 'revolves around' is the correct collocation with 'fulcrum'

given.plan: Identify the idiom 'revolve around' as the standard English expression for something that is centred on or driven by a concept; check that no other option forms a grammatically and semantically correct phrase with 'around the fulcrum of trust'.

steps.title: Understand the metaphor
steps.apply: A fulcrum is a pivot point. The sentence says the business model [verb] around this pivot. Physically, objects 'revolve' around a pivot — so 'revolves around' is the natural fit.
steps.note: Options B (plays), C (sinks), D (bursts) do not collocate with 'around the fulcrum' in standard English.

steps.title: Eliminate distractors
steps.apply: 'Plays around' means to toy with something casually (negative connotation). 'Sinks around' and 'bursts around' are not standard English phrases. Only 'revolves around' is idiomatic and conveys that trust is the central pivot of the business model.
steps.note: Idiom check: 'revolve around an idea' = be fundamentally based on that idea.

solution.result: The correct answer is A. revolves — 'The search engine's business model revolves around the fulcrum of trust.'

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas are required; this is a vocabulary and idiom question.

questions.question:
A court is to a judge as _________ is to a teacher.

A. a student  B. a punishment  C. a syllabus  D. a school
questions.to_find: The word that bears the same relationship to 'teacher' as 'court' does to 'judge'

understand.plain: This is an analogy question. A court is the workplace or institution where a judge performs their professional duties. By the same logic, the answer must be the institution where a teacher performs their professional duties. A school is to a teacher what a court is to a judge.

keywords.explain: A comparison showing that two pairs share the same type of relationship
keywords.example: Hospital : Doctor :: Court : Judge — both are professional workplaces.

keywords.explain: The relationship where a specific building/body is the official setting for a professional role
keywords.example: Parliament is to a legislator as a court is to a judge.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed for this analogy question.

given.aim: Find the word X such that 'X : teacher' mirrors the 'court : judge' relationship

terms.meaning: The official institution where a judge presides and dispenses justice
terms.example: The Supreme Court is the highest court in India.
terms.connects: Workplace/institution of the judge — the first element of the analogy

terms.meaning: The professional who works in a court
terms.example: A judge interprets law and delivers verdicts.
terms.connects: Professional role at the court

terms.meaning: The professional who imparts education
terms.example: A teacher conducts classes and assesses students.
terms.connects: The second professional role — we need their institutional counterpart

given.plan: Identify the relationship as 'institution where professional works', then apply it: the institution where a teacher works is a school.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.apply: Court : Judge = [Institution] : [Professional who works there]. A court is the dedicated professional environment of a judge.
steps.note: The relationship is not 'tool of the trade' or 'outcome of work' — it is specifically the workplace/institution.

steps.title: Apply to the second pair
steps.apply: Teacher's professional institution = school. Check options: A. a student (student is taught by a teacher, not an institution), B. a punishment (outcome, not institution), C. a syllabus (tool/curriculum, not institution), D. a school (institution where teacher works). Answer: D.
steps.note: A school is to a teacher exactly as a court is to a judge.

solution.result: The correct answer is D. a school — a school is the professional institution of a teacher, just as a court is the professional institution of a judge.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas required; this is a verbal analogy question.

questions.question:
The ____________ is too high for it to be considered _____________.

A. fair / fare  B. faer / fair  C. fare / fare  D. fare / fair
questions.to_find: The correct pair of homophones to fill both blanks in the sentence

understand.plain: This question tests knowledge of homophones — words that sound the same but have different spellings and meanings. 'Fare' means a price charged for travel or food offered, while 'fair' means reasonable or just. The sentence 'The fare is too high for it to be considered fair' uses both words correctly: the first blank refers to a price (fare) and the second refers to being reasonable (fair).

keywords.explain: A price charged for transportation (e.g., bus fare, taxi fare) or food served
keywords.example: The train fare from Delhi to Mumbai has increased this year.

keywords.explain: Adjective meaning just, reasonable, or equitable
keywords.example: It is not fair to charge so much for a short ride.

keywords.explain: Words that are pronounced the same but differ in spelling and meaning
keywords.example: fair/fare, there/their/they're, to/too/two are classic homophone pairs.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; this is a homophone vocabulary question.

given.aim: Choose the correct pair of homophones (fare, fair) to make the sentence grammatically and semantically correct

terms.meaning: Needs a noun that can be 'too high' — a price or cost
terms.example: The bus fare is too high.
terms.connects: Points to 'fare' (a price) as the correct word

terms.meaning: Needs an adjective meaning reasonable or just
terms.example: The price was not considered fair by the passengers.
terms.connects: Points to 'fair' (reasonable) as the correct word

terms.meaning: 'Faer' is not a standard English word
terms.example: Option B (faer/fair) is invalid because 'faer' does not exist.
terms.connects: Eliminates option B immediately

given.plan: Eliminate non-words (faer), then use semantic context: 'too high' requires a noun meaning price (fare) and 'considered [blank]' requires an adjective meaning reasonable (fair).

steps.title: Eliminate non-words and same-word options
steps.apply: Option B has 'faer' which is not a real word — eliminated. Option C repeats 'fare/fare': 'The fare is too high for it to be considered fare' — 'fare' is a noun (price/food), not an adjective meaning reasonable, so the second blank is wrong.
steps.note: Two blanks need two different words with different meanings.

steps.title: Test Option D: fare / fair
steps.apply: 'The fare is too high for it to be considered fair.' — 'fare' (price) fills blank 1; 'fair' (reasonable/just) fills blank 2. The sentence is grammatically correct and semantically coherent.
steps.note: Option A (fair/fare) reverses the order: 'The fair is too high for it to be considered fare' — 'fair' as a noun means a festival/carnival whose height makes no sense, and 'considered fare' is grammatically awkward.

solution.result: The correct answer is D. fare / fair — 'The fare is too high for it to be considered fair.'

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas required; this is a homophones vocabulary question.

questions.question:
We reached the station late, and _______ missed the train.

A. near  B. nearly  C. utterly  D. mostly
questions.to_find: The most appropriate adverb to indicate that the train was almost (but not quite) missed

understand.plain: The context requires an adverb that means 'almost' or 'by a small margin'. The sentence implies that they arrived late but still managed to catch the train — they came very close to missing it but did not. 'Nearly missed' is the standard English phrase for this near-miss situation. 'Near' is an adjective/preposition, not an adverb. 'Utterly' and 'mostly' do not fit the 'almost' meaning.

keywords.explain: Adverb meaning almost, very close to a result but not quite
keywords.example: She nearly fell off the bicycle but caught her balance.

keywords.explain: 'Near' is an adjective or preposition; 'nearly' is the adverb form used to modify verbs
keywords.example: We were near the station. (adjective) / We nearly missed it. (adverb)

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed for this adverb selection question.

given.aim: Choose the adverb that correctly conveys 'almost missed the train' in the given sentence

terms.meaning: They arrived at the station after the scheduled time, making it probable they would miss the train
terms.example: Reaching the station late but still catching the train implies a narrow escape.
terms.connects: Establishes the 'near-miss' scenario requiring 'nearly'

terms.meaning: The blank modifies the verb 'missed', so an adverb is grammatically required
terms.example: 'Near missed' is incorrect grammar; 'nearly missed' is correct.
terms.connects: Eliminates 'near' (adjective/preposition, not adverb)

terms.meaning: 'Utterly' and 'mostly' imply full or large-scale completion, which contradicts the 'but we caught it' implication
terms.example: Utterly missed = completely missed; mostly missed = largely missed — both mean they did NOT catch the train.
terms.connects: Eliminates options C and D

given.plan: Identify that the blank needs an adverb meaning 'almost'; check grammar (adverb, not adjective) and semantics (near-miss, not complete miss).

steps.title: Determine grammatical class needed
steps.apply: The blank modifies the verb 'missed' — an adverb is required. 'Near' is an adjective/preposition in standard usage; it cannot modify a verb here. This eliminates option A.
steps.note: In formal English, 'near' cannot be used adverbially to mean 'almost'; 'nearly' is the correct adverb form.

steps.title: Check semantic meaning of remaining options
steps.apply: 'Utterly missed' = completely failed to catch the train (negative extreme). 'Mostly missed' = largely missed. Both imply they did NOT board the train. 'Nearly missed' = came very close to missing but still caught it — the intended meaning.
steps.note: The sentence structure 'reached late, and nearly missed' suggests they barely made it in time.

solution.result: The correct answer is B. nearly — 'We reached the station late, and nearly missed the train' conveys the near-miss scenario correctly.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas required; this is a grammar and vocabulary question.

questions.question:
If '-->' denotes increasing order of intensity, then the meaning of the words [dry --> arid --> parched] is analogous to [diet --> fast --> ________ ]. Which one of the given options is appropriate to fill the blank?

A. starve  B. reject  C. feast  D. deny
questions.to_find: The word that follows 'fast' in increasing intensity of food deprivation, analogous to 'parched' following 'arid'

understand.plain: The question uses an intensity analogy. 'Dry -> arid -> parched' represents increasing degrees of dryness/lack of water: dry is mildly dry, arid is very dry, parched is extremely dry. By analogy, 'diet -> fast -> ?' represents increasing degrees of food restriction: dieting is mild reduction, fasting is complete abstinence for a period, and the next level (extreme food deprivation) is 'starve'.

keywords.explain: A word sequence where each successive word represents a more extreme degree of the same concept
keywords.example: warm -> hot -> scorching shows increasing heat intensity.

keywords.explain: To suffer or die from lack of food; the most extreme form of food deprivation
keywords.example: Without food aid, people in famine-stricken regions starve.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed for this word-intensity analogy question.

given.aim: Identify the word that represents the most extreme degree of food restriction, beyond 'fast'

terms.meaning: Increasing lack of moisture: slightly dry -> very dry -> extremely dry/thirsty
terms.example: A parched desert is more severe than merely an arid one.
terms.connects: Template for the intensity pattern to be matched

terms.meaning: Increasing food restriction: reducing food intake -> complete abstinence from food for a period
terms.example: Intermittent fasting is more extreme than simply dieting.
terms.connects: First two elements of the second series; the blank is the third, most extreme element

terms.meaning: To be severely deprived of food, suffering extreme hunger or risk of death
terms.example: The prisoners were left to starve in the dungeon.
terms.connects: Most extreme food deprivation — matches the role of 'parched' in the first series

given.plan: Map each step in the dryness series to the food-restriction series: dry=diet (mild), arid=fast (moderate), parched=? (extreme). The extreme end of food deprivation is 'starve'.

steps.title: Map the analogy structure
steps.apply: dry : diet = mild level. arid : fast = moderate level. parched : ? = extreme level. We need the word for extreme food deprivation beyond fasting.
steps.note: 'Parched' means severely lacking water to the point of great suffering — its food analogue must convey severe food deprivation.

steps.title: Evaluate options
steps.apply: A. starve — extreme food deprivation, fits perfectly. B. reject — means to refuse/dismiss, no food-deprivation intensity. C. feast — means to eat abundantly, opposite direction. D. deny — means to refuse, not an intensity of deprivation. Answer: A.
steps.note: Feast (C) is actually the opposite of the pattern; starve is the correct escalation.

solution.result: The correct answer is A. starve — the intensity series is diet (mild restriction) -> fast (moderate abstinence) -> starve (extreme deprivation), mirroring dry -> arid -> parched.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas required; this is a word-intensity analogy question.

questions.question:
In the given text, the blanks are numbered (i)-(iv). Select the best match for all the blanks.

Steve was advised to keep his head (i) before heading (ii) to bat; for, while he had a head (iii) batting, he could only do so with a cool head (iv) his shoulders.

A. (i) down  (ii) down  (iii) on  (iv) for
B. (i) on  (ii) out  (iii) for  (iv) on
C. (i) down  (ii) out  (iii) for  (iv) on
D. (i) down  (ii) out  (iii) on  (iv) for
questions.to_find: The correct set of prepositions/particles to fill all four blanks using idiomatic English phrases

understand.plain: This question tests knowledge of fixed English idioms involving the word 'head'. Each blank requires a specific preposition or particle to complete a standard idiom: 'keep one's head down' (stay low-profile/focused), 'head out' (to go somewhere), 'have a head for' (to have a natural talent for), and 'a cool head on one's shoulders' (calmness and good judgment). The cricket batting context provides semantic clues.

keywords.explain: Idiom meaning to stay focused, avoid distraction, or maintain a low profile
keywords.example: Before the big match, players are told to keep their heads down and concentrate.

keywords.explain: Phrasal verb meaning to leave or go in a particular direction
keywords.example: He headed out to the field to start his innings.

keywords.explain: Idiom meaning to have a natural talent or aptitude for something
keywords.example: She has a head for numbers — maths comes easily to her.

keywords.term: a cool head on one's shoulders
keywords.explain: Idiom meaning to be calm, sensible, and level-headed
keywords.example: A good batsman keeps a cool head on his shoulders under pressure.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; this is a multi-blank idiom-completion question.

given.aim: Match each blank with the correct preposition or particle to form four standard English idioms in the cricket batting context

terms.term: blank (i): keep his head ___ before batting
terms.meaning: Needs to form 'keep one's head down' = stay focused and avoid distraction
terms.example: Steve kept his head down and practised his technique.
terms.connects: Confirms particle 'down' for blank (i)

terms.meaning: Needs to form 'head out' = to go somewhere / set off
terms.example: He headed out to the crease for his innings.
terms.connects: Confirms particle 'out' for blank (ii)

terms.meaning: Needs to form 'have a head for [something]' = have natural talent for it
terms.example: Steve had a head for batting — he was a natural.
terms.connects: Confirms preposition 'for' for blank (iii)

terms.term: blank (iv): a cool head ___ his shoulders
terms.meaning: Needs to form 'a cool head on one's shoulders' = calm, sensible disposition
terms.example: He played brilliantly because he had a cool head on his shoulders.
terms.connects: Confirms preposition 'on' for blank (iv)

given.plan: Identify each standard idiom: (i) keep head down, (ii) head out, (iii) head for [talent], (iv) head on shoulders. The only option matching down/out/for/on is C.

steps.title: Identify each idiom
steps.apply: (i) 'keep his head down' = stay calm and focused before batting. (ii) 'heading out to bat' = going to the crease. (iii) 'a head for batting' = natural talent for batting. (iv) 'a cool head on his shoulders' = calm and level-headed.
steps.note: All four idioms are established fixed expressions in English.

steps.title: Match with options
steps.apply: Required: (i)=down, (ii)=out, (iii)=for, (iv)=on. Check options: A gives (ii)=down — wrong ('heading down' not idiomatic for going somewhere). B gives (i)=on — 'keep head on' is not the standard idiom for focus. D gives (iv)=for — 'cool head for shoulders' is non-standard. Only C matches: down/out/for/on.
steps.note: Option D is close but blank (iv) 'on' vs 'for' is the distinguishing factor; the correct idiom is 'head on shoulders', not 'head for shoulders'.

solution.result: The correct answer is C: (i) down, (ii) out, (iii) for, (iv) on — forming the four idioms: keep head down, head out, head for [talent], cool head on shoulders.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas required; this is a multi-blank idiom-identification question.

questions.question:
If '-->' denotes increasing order of intensity, then the meaning of the words [walk --> jog --> sprint] is analogous to [bothered --> ________ --> daunted].

Which one of the given options is appropriate to fill the blank?

A. phased  B. phrased  C. fazed  D. fused
questions.to_find: The word of intermediate intensity between 'bothered' and 'daunted' in a scale of being disturbed or intimidated

understand.plain: The question uses an intensity analogy. 'Walk -> jog -> sprint' shows increasing speed of movement. By analogy, 'bothered -> ? -> daunted' shows increasing emotional disturbance/intimidation. 'Bothered' is mildly troubled; 'daunted' is strongly discouraged/intimidated. The middle word must represent a moderate level of being disturbed — 'fazed' means noticeably unsettled or disconcerted, fitting perfectly between mildly bothered and greatly daunted.

keywords.explain: Adjective/verb meaning disturbed, disconcerted, or unsettled by something; more than bothered but less extreme than daunted
keywords.example: The noisy crowd did not faze the experienced speaker.

keywords.explain: Feeling intimidated, discouraged, or overwhelmed by something difficult
keywords.example: She was daunted by the sheer size of the task ahead.

keywords.explain: 'Phased' relates to phases/stages; 'fazed' means unsettled or disturbed — a common confusion in English
keywords.example: The change was phased in gradually. / Nothing fazed the calm negotiator.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed for this word-intensity analogy question.

given.aim: Find the word of intermediate emotional disturbance intensity between 'bothered' (mild) and 'daunted' (extreme)

terms.meaning: Low -> medium -> high intensity of locomotion speed
terms.example: A sprint is far more intense than a walk; jog sits in the middle.
terms.connects: Template for the intensity progression pattern

terms.meaning: Mildly troubled or annoyed
terms.example: She was bothered by the noise but still managed to concentrate.
terms.connects: Low-intensity end of the disturbance scale — matches 'walk'

terms.meaning: Strongly discouraged or intimidated — feeling overwhelmed
terms.example: He was daunted by the mountain of paperwork ahead.
terms.connects: High-intensity end — matches 'sprint'; the blank must match 'jog'

given.plan: The blank is the medium-intensity word in the disturbance series. Evaluate: phased (stages, unrelated), phrased (expressed in words, unrelated), fazed (disconcerted — fits as medium disturbance), fused (joined together, unrelated).

steps.title: Establish the intensity scale
steps.apply: bothered (mild disturbance) < ? (moderate disturbance) < daunted (strong intimidation). The blank corresponds to 'jog' in the locomotion series — a clearly elevated but not maximum level of disturbance.
steps.note: The disturbance scale: bothered < fazed < daunted maps to walk < jog < sprint.

steps.title: Eliminate incorrect options
steps.apply: A. phased — means divided into phases/stages, not related to emotional disturbance. B. phrased — means expressed in words, unrelated. D. fused — means joined or merged, unrelated. C. fazed — means disconcerted or unsettled; a moderate level of emotional disturbance. Answer: C.
steps.note: Note the spelling difference: 'fazed' (unsettled) vs 'phased' (staged) — a common confusion tested here.

solution.result: The correct answer is C. fazed — 'bothered -> fazed -> daunted' mirrors 'walk -> jog -> sprint' in increasing intensity of emotional disturbance.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas required; this is a word-intensity analogy question testing knowledge of 'fazed' vs 'phased'.
